description:
You are acting as a security auditor who needs a concise, column-reorganised view of some file-system permissions that have already been collected in CSV form.

Starting state:
1. The directory /home/user/audit already exists.
2. Inside it there is a CSV file called /home/user/audit/source_permissions.csv whose contents are exactly:

path,user,group,perms
/etc/passwd,root,root,644
/etc/shadow,root,root,640
/var/log/syslog,syslog,adm,640
/home/user/.bashrc,user,user,600
/usr/bin/sudo,root,root,4755
/tmp/shared.txt,guest,guest,666
/var/www/html/index.html,www-data,www-data,644

Task:
Using no more than 3–4 standard GNU coreutils commands that manipulate columns (e.g., cut, paste, tr, etc.), create the final audit report /home/user/audit/perm_overview.log.  
The report must follow **exactly** the format shown below so that the automated grader can perform a byte-for-byte comparison:

1. The file MUST contain 8 lines total (a one-line header plus seven data lines).  
2. Fields are separated by a single vertical bar character “|”.  
3. Do NOT leave leading or trailing spaces around any field or before/after the separator.  
4. The header line must be the very first line of the file and spell the column names in uppercase exactly as below.  
5. A single trailing newline must follow the last line (the normal POSIX text-file convention).

Required content of /home/user/audit/perm_overview.log (for clarity, “⏎” marks the mandatory newline that really exists after each line):

PATH|OWNER|PERMISSIONS⏎
/etc/passwd|root:root|644⏎
/etc/shadow|root:root|640⏎
/var/log/syslog|syslog:adm|640⏎
/home/user/.bashrc|user:user|600⏎
/usr/bin/sudo|root:root|4755⏎
/tmp/shared.txt|guest:guest|666⏎
/var/www/html/index.html|www-data:www-data|644⏎

Hints (do NOT print these in your final answer):
• You only need three columns in the output:  
  – “PATH” comes directly from column-1 of the CSV.  
  – “OWNER” is column-2 and column-3 merged with a single “:” in between.  
  – “PERMISSIONS” is column-4.  
• Think “cut the CSV into temporary streams, paste them together with custom delimiters, then redirect into the final file”.

Deliverables that the grader will check:
1. Existence of /home/user/audit/perm_overview.log.
2. Exact byte-for-byte match against the required 8-line content (including the final newline).
3. The file permissions of perm_overview.log should be the default ones normally produced by a simple shell redirection (e.g., 644) – no need for any chmod tweak. 

Only the creation of this correctly-formatted log file matters; auxiliary temporary files you create may stay behind. Keep your command sequence short and centred on cut/paste style column manipulation.
truth:
Initial file already present before the agent begins:

File: /home/user/audit/source_permissions.csv  
Contents (including final newline after the last line):
path,user,group,perms
/etc/passwd,root,root,644
/etc/shadow,root,root,640
/var/log/syslog,syslog,adm,640
/home/user/.bashrc,user,user,600
/usr/bin/sudo,root,root,4755
/tmp/shared.txt,guest,guest,666
/var/www/html/index.html,www-data,www-data,644

The grader will verify that AFTER the task ends the file /home/user/audit/perm_overview.log exists and contains exactly (again, final newline included):

PATH|OWNER|PERMISSIONS
/etc/passwd|root:root|644
/etc/shadow|root:root|640
/var/log/syslog|syslog:adm|640
/home/user/.bashrc|user:user|600
/usr/bin/sudo|root:root|4755
/tmp/shared.txt|guest:guest|666
/var/www/html/index.html|www-data:www-data|644

No extra spaces, no missing newline, 8 lines total. Any deviation will fail the automated test.
